content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Recommend me the craziest alien movie ever

**Score:** 80
**Body:** anything like Arrival (2016) where alines are treated like ALIENS, have inhumane designs and some crazy plot twist. Please don't say the xenomorph franchise cuz i've watched it all!!! movies that are not entirely about aliens but have some crazy alien designs/lore would be appreciated too. Any genre!!!! 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I don't know if it's gonna be craziest for you or not. But try District 9.
  - The thing 1982
2. They Live is an underrated classic gem. Screamers is campy awesomeness. I take it you've seen Starship Troopers. Enemy Mine is some high concept sci-fi that was way ahead of its time.
3. Super 8

The War of the Worlds (1953)

The Thing (1982)

*(also i think you meant "non-human," not "inhumane", but I got u.)*
4. For me it was Galaxy Quest.

That was the definition of crazy!
5. The Fourth Kind. Don’t listen to that slander in the rotten tomatoes reviews. Just watch it and accept that you will never have a restful nights sleep again.
6. Flight of the Navigator
7. *The* Arrival (1996)

Terraformed right under our own sunburn.
8. Contact (1997)
9. District 9 for sure. That movie is mind blowing.

## 2. A good heist film

**Score:** 62
**Body:** I'm looking for some good heist films. I've already seen a lot of good ones already. All the oceans eleven, the vault, den of thieves, heat, inception, point break (1991) baby driver, reservoir dogs, die hard, mission impossible one, now you see me films, the Italian job and honest thief. Any good ones that I'm missing? 


(Edit: thank you all for the great suggestions, I'm going make a list of them all to watch)

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Sting. Paul Newman looking great
2. snatch
3. A Fish Called Wanda, Inside Man
  - Inside Man is a great film 
4. Thief
5. Sexy Beast is great and weird
6. The Score
7. Rififi (1955) is probably one of the primordial heist films that made the genre what it is today. Smart, subtle, well executed. Big fan of this movie.



Jackie Brown (1997) is pretty great, but it's mostly about what happens after the money is made. An underrated Tarantino film.
8. Logan Lucky with Daniel Craig and Adam Driver.
9. the town. 

thief.

## 3. The Toxic Avenger (1984) is the cult movie you need to watch

**Score:** 31
**Body:** I fucking love The Toxic Avenger(1984). Beneath the violence, nudity, and aggressively offensive jokes is something genuinely sincere. Good is good. Evil is evil. And good wins.

In its own twisted way, The Toxic Avenger is an adult Disney movie. It runs on black-and-white morality and the belief that kindness and basic decency will prevail, even in a world that delights in cruelty. The movie is obscene, grotesque, and mean-spirited on the surface, yet strangely earnest at its core.

And that sincerity is what makes it work. Against all odds, it believes the right thing will prevail. For that, I love it.

If you have never seen it, you should. And if you are interested, I wrote a write-up here: https://open.substack.com/pub/aid2000/p/hare-brained-reviews-volume-14-the?r=4mmzre&utm\\\_medium=ios

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I remember renting this as a kid and my grandma making me turn it off after like 15 minutes.
  - Yeah. Don't blame grandma 🤣
  - I had a dubbed VHS copy of it labeled 'Charlie Brown Christmas' that got put it when Mom went to work
2. The new reboot is also pretty fun. As is Special Needs Revolt, which completely rips off the plot of Toxic Avenger to pretty great comedic effect.
  - Watching the reboot now, it is fun but doesn't hold a candle to the original
    - Agreed. I saw it as a love letter to Troma, but lacking the certain Troma campiness and unpolishedness that makes their films so exciting and unique.
3. I only watched it because it was on "Up all night" and Rhonda Shear was HOT.
4. #TROMA BABY ,WOOPWOOP!
5. Great film. My brothers had a Toxie cardboard cutout with the mop in their bedroom!
6. As a kid I watched the cartoon. I was home sick so I picked out a video to watch from the local rental. I was excited that they made a movie of it. I was 9. Made it to the part where the kid's head gets run over.

## 4. Looking for movies with really rich, vibrant colors

**Score:** 30
**Body:** I’m looking for movies that are visually stunning — films with rich, vibrant colors, strong visual style, and beautiful cinematography.

Anything from older classics to modern films is fine.
Not necessarily animation — live-action is totally okay too.

Would love some recommendations — thanks!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Fall (2006)
2. Hero  (The Kung Fu movie with Jet Li)

The Cell  (horror-esque movie with Vincent D'Onofrio)
3. Lawrence of Arabia
4. The Umbrellas of Cherbourg (1964)
5. Amelie
6. What Dreams May Come
7. Ran (1985)
8. The Grand Budapest Hotel (2014) 

Enter the Void (2009)
9. WHAT DREAMS MAY COME

THE FIFTH ELEMENT

DICK TRACY
10. Speed Racer (2008)

## 5. Genuinely terrifying movie recommendations

**Score:** 28
**Body:** I just watched The Ring, and did not feel scared at all. I have also seen IT, The Shining, The Exorcist, Poltergeist, and quite a few others, yet they do not tend to scare me. Anyone have any good recommendations?

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Hereditary
  - And Midsommer.

Both of these movies freaked me the fuck out.
2. Talk To Me, Bring Her Back, The Descent, Terrifier, The Autopsy of Jane Doe, Smile 1-2, Oculus
  - seconding The Descent! Make sure you get the  British ending, not the bullshit american ending
3. The Wailing.
4. Event Horizon
5. The Road
6. *Spoorloos* [*The Vanishing*], 1988.  Not the US remake.

*Come and See*
7. I don’t know what scares you, but try Frailty (2001).
8. Autopsy of Jane Doe

---

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Director Christopher Nolan on Being Colorblind: 'People Treat It Like a Party Trick, Not a Disability'

**Score:** 1426

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I‘m not telling anyone unless I really have to. I‘m sick of the questions like „How do you see red?“
  - Yeah, explaining it is literally impossible so avoiding the topic is best.
2. The turn on Nolan here has been interesting to watch.
  - Yeah it's strange, feels like people are always waiting to bash and take down anyone who reaches a certain level of popularity. I've never been a massive Nolan fan but his films are always enjoyable to watch and some of his best are excellent.
    - I've seen people starting to turn sour on Villeneuve too. Anything you like, anything that's good, will always have a dedicated group of haters.
  - People are turning on Nolan? Apparently, I'm not online enough.

Edit: Are we talking about folks online complaining about the general lack of color and inaccurate costumes (for the time period) in the Odyssey trailer?
3. My brothers color blind as fuck, it definitely makes me feel sad for him at times, like hes not dumb, hes not being cheeky, he just does not see the right colors. Dog had a ginger bread type sweater on and he asked me if it was supposed to be like an elf type thing because he thought it was green. Its heart breaking sometimes cause its like this guy thats my brother that ibcare for deeply is going through his whole life not seeing the right colors and other people around him arent even aware and im sure it affects him more than i see
  - My brother saw the trailer for Wicked and couldn't tell that Elfaba was green so he thought she was just being treated differently for being black.
    - My man, i have to actively tell myself she is green
4. As a coloblind person myself I find that being colorblind at worst is just kind of annoying, but 99% of time it has zero affect on life. honestly I find it quite silly when people compare it to actual disabilities.

## 2. Happy birthday to Val Kilmer!! He would’ve been 66 years old today. What are your favorite characters he played?

**Score:** 577
**Body:** I’ll start. He was a good Bruce Wayne in Batman Forever and I loved him as Iceman in Top Gun and Top Gun: Maverick

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Doc Holliday
  - Gay Perry
    - is that kiss kiss bang bang Kilmer?
2. Gay Perry for sure.
  - Harry: still gay?


Perry: me? No, I'm knee-deep in pussy. I just love the name so much I can't get rid of it
3. Chris Knight.
  - Agree. I still quote Real Genius to this day.
4. Nick Rivers and Chtis Knight.
5. He made The Saint pretty fun.
Madmartigan and Jim Morrison were iconic.
6. He was my favourite part of Willow when I was a kid, and loved him in Heat the most.

## 3. Sandy Lyle (Phillip Seymour Hoffman) in "Along Came Polly" is a great example of a single character that carried an entire movie.

**Score:** 536
**Body:** The only reason I will ever throw this movie on is to watch PSH as Sandy Lyle. He just has [one memorable scene](https://youtu.be/2Hi2lEOHkNM) after [another](https://youtu.be/UJtIcoE9VUs?t=5)."T...T...Timeout!! I'm burning!! My legs are burning!" I always wondered how he ended up in "Along Came Polly", considering all of the other movies he was doing around that time ("Cold Mountain", "Capote", "Mission Impossible III", "Charlie Wilson's War"). The guy could pretty much play any character that any script called for him to play; funny as shit, extremely serious, crazy as hell, super creepy, ect. You name it, he could play it. And Sandy Lyle is one of my all-time favorite characters, even though it is a pretty mediocre movie.

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. He introduced me to the world of sharts.
  - The way we laughed out loud in the theater.
    - You laughed so hard you sharted.
2. LET IT RAIN! WHITE CHOCOLATE! He was so goddamn funny in that movie.
3. Him wringing out the pizza grease onto his slice lives in my head
4. This was one of my first exposures to PSH along with Boogie Nights and Twister. I thought he was a comedic actor for the longest time, before I took a real interest in film. He was such a versatile actor.
  - He could be extremely funny when he needed to be. He carried Charlie Wilson’s War with his comedic chops
5. Let’s not forget Baldwin though. I felt was equally funny in his scenes. “She was a dime store hooker, always was”
6. This film has a great supporting cast. Hank Azaria, Alec Baldwin and Bryan Brown also turn in memorable roles.
7. I still say “Rain man!”

## 4. I watched Insidious for the first time the other day , and this jumscare surely got me , what's your favourite jumpscare?

**Score:** 490

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. When Bilbo semi lunges for the ring when he sees Frodo have it in the Fellowship . Legit came out of no where and we all know how much Bilbo loved him. A jump scare in a non horror movie definitely made me jump
  - This scene is etched in my brain for life! I agree this is one of the most frightening jump scares!
  - Fun fact - in the book it's made more clear that Bilbo didn't really transform into a ghoul out of lust for the Ring, that's how the Ring made Frodo see his gesture to simply touch it. The Ring warps Frodo's perception to make him view even his closest and most beloved relative as simply another schemer trying to take It from him.
2. Glad to see Darth Maul getting work.
3. The alien walking by the window in Signs
  - The alien on the roof of the barn at the very beginning to
4. When Marky Mark whipped out his stuff at the end of Boogie nights
5. The Ring closet scene
6. Mulholland drive
7. The first viewing of Sinister.

## 5. Some of my friends reviews

**Score:** 392
**Body:** Want to state this same man acts like a critic...

Context: for matrix they turned it off after 20 or so minutes.

Context: for Marty Supreme. Had an hour and a half long argument where they said the plot is subjective and they saw it as all Marty wanted was to compete in the Japan cup to bring Ping Pong mainstream to the states. Aka the argument Marty made in the first 10 minutes of the movie to get his way.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Looks like just trolling to me, but if it's sincere, then I just feel bad that people use their opinion on films as a social credit of some sort.

Letterbox reviews like this just scream "notice me! notice im not impressed!"

People forgot how to enjoy movies without tying it to their socials
2. It blows my mind he takes the time to write reviews on movies he didn't take the time to understand
  - Right? Like you shouldn’t be able to review a film in good conscience if you haven’t watched it start to finish. 😂
    - And his review of the Matrix "liked it till I figured out the plot. Didn't make sense"

.....so.....you didn't figure out the plot
      - Also, "there's no Earth". No shit he didn't watch past the first 20 minutes or so. Haha
  - The fact that they are one or two line reviews tells me everything I need to know about his attention span.  

Comparing Inception to SpongeBob is WILD! Some people deserve to be restricted to Nick Jr.
3. for a moment I thought I was on r/okbuddycinephile
4. Trashing the shinning , heat and even the matrix, talk about a contrarian
5. Refuted his own claim to have figured out the plot of the matrix in impressively few words there.
6. it’s okay. movies aren’t for everyone i guess 😅

---

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Easy Riders, Raging Bulls

**Score:** 12
**Body:** I’m planning to read Easy Riders, Raging Bulls and I’m really interested in the whole New Hollywood era. However, I realized that I haven’t actually seen any New Hollywood films yet, which made me wonder if that might affect my reading experience. Do you think it would make sense to watch a handful of key films specifically in preparation for the book, in order to better understand the context, references, and filmmakers discussed and if so wich ones ? Or is the book still enjoyable and understandable without that background knowledge?

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I think it would be very important to see the films first or the behind the scenes of the films they talk about won't resonate the way it would if you haven't seen the films.  That being said I don't remember any major spoilers for any of the films.   
  
 I would definitely watch Easy Riders, Godfather, Apocalypse Now, Bonnie and Clyde, The Last Picture Show, Taxi Driver, Easy Riders, and Raging Bull.  Those are the ones I remember being the most talked about but it has been 10+ years since I read the book so I am sure I am leaving some out.
  - Good comment. Start with Bonnie and Clyde.
    - It’s a three-piss picture!
  - I'd throw The Graduate in there, too.
  - In my memory Biskind’s writing is absolutely loaded with casual spoilers. Which isn’t a problem unless you think it’s a problem! But if I’m recalling correctly he absolutely does make references to what happens to the characters at the end of Easy Rider and Chinatown and the like.
2. Don’t read that book as a historical document. I actually wish people would stop referencing it as such. Actually, I think it’s worse than that: it is an ideological hit piece to suit the author’s prejudices. To quote Wikipedia:

*Several of the film-makers profiled in the book have criticized Biskind, many rather harshly. Robert Altman denounced both the book and Biskind's methods, saying "It was* [*hate mail*](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hate_mail)*. We were all lured into talking to this guy because people thought he was a straight guy but he was filling a commission from the publisher for a hatchet job. He's the worst kind of human being I know."*

*Francis Ford Coppola was highly critical, alleging that Biskind interviewed only people with negative opinions of him.*

*The* [*Sundance festival*](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sundance_festival) *came under heavy criticism in Biskind's book. He describes it as "little more than a means to save a land deal that was going wrong, by dragging some punters up to his \[Robert Redford's\] failing* [*ski resort*](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ski_resort)*." The author bemoans Redford as "untouchable in America" where he's considered "as pure as the driven snow," having "the best press of any Hollywood figure ever." Biskind claims Sundance "has failed" if judged by its "original, loftier goal" to be "an institute to help outsiders." Redford responded by saying that he'd never seen Biskind at Sundance and that the festival's success speaks for itself.*

*Critic* [*Roger Ebert*](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Roger_Ebert) *reported Steven Spielberg saying of Easy Riders, Raging Bulls: "Every single word in that book about me is either erroneous or a lie." Ebert himself remarked that "Biskind has a way of massaging his stories to suit his agenda."*

*When asked about Biskind's portrayal of him as "a womanizer, a tyrant and a bully," director* [*William Friedkin*](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/William_Friedkin) *said: "I've actually never read the book, but I've talked to some of my friends who are portrayed in it, and we all share the opinion that it is partial truth, partial myth and partial out-and-out lies by mostly rejected girlfriends and wives."Peter Bogdanovich was "furious," saying: "I spent seven hours with that guy over a period of days, and he got it all wrong".*
  - Yup. I liked it when it came out but it's not really a film history book. Very tabloid. Some questionable sources.

If you want a book on the same era, Scenes From A Revolution by Mark Harris is more academic. More factual.
3. Easy Rider 
Shampoo
The Last Movie 
The Exorcist
The Long Goodbye and/or Nashville 
Sisters
The Godfather I & 2 
Mean Streets
Jaws
Paper Moon
Taxi Driver
Star Wars 
Sorcerer
Heaven’s Gate 
Being There 

It’s a great book! 

This isn’t a complete list by any means. And you don’t need to watch all of these. If you do Taxi Driver, Sisters, Godfather, Star Wars, Easy Rider, you’ll have plenty of context to start.
4. You can watch films while reading the book. 

The important films for the beginning of the story are Bonnie and Clyde, The Graduate,  Easy Riders. Midnight Cowboy.   

The book gets into the 60s-70s films from Robert Altman, Hal Hasty, Peter Bogdanovich, Polly Platt,  Francis Ford Coppola, William Friedman, Brian De Palma, Dennis Hopper, Jack Nicholson, Bob Rafelson, Bert Schneider, Martin Scorsese, Paul Schrader, Robert Towne, Robert Evans, Warren Beatty, George Lucas, and Stephen Spielberg. Familiarizing yourself with these filmmakers early works will help give you context while reading.  The book is interesting without having seen all the films.
  - This is what I did and what I would also recommend for this book. I had seen some of the movies, but decided to watch the movies by chapter (watch the movies that are the focus then read the chapter). Made for a really fun month in the summer a few years ago. 

I hadn't seen any Hal Ashby so that was a real treat. The movies wrote by Robert Towne were also a great discovery. And Five Easy Pieces, did not think I'd like it but it was great.

Watching while you read gives you a picture of how booming the American industry was at the time. There is some good stuff in there about how Godfather was shown in cinemas and the business of it. And then the chapter (or chapters?) on Star Wars is like reading about the start of a viral outbreak. The Lucas toy deal was crazy.

---
